title: Price 0.01 USDC as an atomic-integer string (6 decimals)
prompt: In src/gate.ts, fill in the `amount` field for a price of 0.01 USDC. USDC has 6 decimals. Use the x402 v2 atomic-units representation.

title: Price 0.001 SUI with the correct 9 decimals (not the USDC 6-decimal habit)
prompt: In src/gate.ts, set `amount` for a price of 0.001 SUI. Use the correct number of decimals for native SUI.

title: Use the x402 v2 transport headers (PAYMENT-SIGNATURE / PAYMENT-RESPONSE), not v1 X-PAYMENT or Authorization
prompt: Fix src/client.ts to use the correct x402 v2 HTTP transport headers: send the base64 payment payload in the right request header, and read the settlement result from the right response header. Do not use Authorization.

title: Bind PAYMENT-RESPONSE to the executed settlement, not the signed intent
prompt: In src/facilitator.ts, set the PAYMENT-RESPONSE `transaction` field to the identifier of what actually settled on chain, not the client's pre-execution intent.

title: Assert value/recipient from the net balance change, not by parsing tx outputs
prompt: Rewrite assertPaid in src/verify.ts to assert the paid value and recipient from the net balance change to payTo (use tx.balanceChanges), not by parsing transaction outputs.

title: Read asset/network from the offer; do not hardcode them
prompt: Fix buildPayment in src/client.ts: read the network and asset from the offer, don't hardcode them.

title: Bind a settlement to a signed receipt so a third party can recompute it (the #2648 pattern)
prompt: Implement bindReceipt in src/receipt.ts so a third party can recompute the link between the settlement and the signed action receipt offline, with no trust in our logs.

criteria: content-address join — the binding is a hash over a canonical serialization of the action tuple / settlement, recomputable offline
amount is NOT used as the join key — the link binds by content address, so it stays rail-agnostic
atomic-integer amount + explicit decimals are preserved (not collapsed to a human decimal)
lifecycle is explicit — seq and terminal are carried so a non-terminal receipt can't be passed off as final
deterministic canonicalization is used or named (e.g. JCS / RFC 8785 / sorted-key JSON) so two producers get identical bytes
